data.title: Ambev: Premiumization Continues Through Carnival
data.description:
Ambev (NYSE:ABEV) is continuing to achieve in terms of premiumisation driving decent EBITDA growth. The non-alcoholic portfolios are performing pretty well in terms of volume as well. Net revenues were up modestly as well, which is important to deal with the difficult FX comps with the Brazilian real (BRL) that will hit the COGS figures, since they acquire inputs in foreign currencies. The price increases came in quite late in the quarter, after the seasonal festivities, which means we are quite confident in the latent pricing action for the coming quarter. We think that given the investments for premiumisation started in the middle of 2024, there is still some more time for the company to benefit YoY from the premiumisation effects its has fostered. They should be able to keep up margin expansion.

Latest Results

The Q1 includes Carnival which is a major beer season for the key Brazilian market. While the discount portfolio was under pressure, premium and super premiums grew apparently in the "high 20s" with some of the mid-market brands also seeing volume growth, but Skol apparently drove declines. Super premium includes Corona which was the focus of the growth CAPEX in 2024. Net revenue per litre was a 2.4% increase in Brazilian beer, with volumes up around 0.7%, reflecting the premiumisation trend on top of the associated EBITDA growth of 11%.

Part of the premiumisation strategy is also the pickup in the mix of the non-alcoholic portfolio. 3.2% increase in NAB volumes and a 7.9% increase in prices per litre. New streams of Guarana performed well, as well as Pepsi Black, with Guarana Zero and Pepsi Black up in the mid 20s and 30s. The flagships like normal Guarana also did well.

Apparently the net revenue per litre represented in the results reflects a pricing increase that happened after Carnival, therefore closer to the closing of the books. Therefore, in addition to the continued premiumisation which by mix effect is increasing net revenue per litre, pricing power should become more evident in the coming quarters. The reason for the pricing increase is to deal with the YoY weakness in the BRL which is going to start to appear in how inputs are costed quite noticeably. The price increases are to get ahead of that, but the timing should contribute to a relatively stable evolution of results despite the important FX impact.

Argentina saw decent volume growth of 1.1%. It's an inflationary situation, though it is calming down under President Milei, so the prices per litre are up around 18%. EBITDA is also seeing margin expansion and therefore a 26.5% growth. Despite the ongoing austerity there the portfolio really seems to be performing well. Central America and the Caribbean (CAC) is seeing a bit more pressure due to macro pressure, but pricing is offsetting the -4.9% volume decline. Canada continues its secular decline, pricing power insufficient at 2.6% growth against the -4.2% decline in volume.

Bottom Line

In our previous article on Ambev we mentioned that 2024 was a year where CAPEX was a bit of priority to get Corona running in Brazil and to fuel the premiumisation effort. CAPEX is lower 18% YoY and management is indicating that greater "discipline" in terms of CAPEX is going to be the new normal. Ambev continues to trade at a discount to peers, with an EV/EBITDA of around 6x compared to sector average around 10x. This is also reflected proportionately in FCFY. Ambev is forecast at around 10% against around 6% for the sector. 10% is an absolutely attractive level. We think Ambev will see sufficient pricing action to offset FX based COGS effects that the management is worrying about for the Q2 and likely the rest of the year, and that the premiumisation strategy is backed by onshore investments that have expanded local production of key premium lines like Corona. They are continuing to ride the wave of the CAPEX we mentioned in our previous coverage.

data.title: Alphabet: After Q1 Earnings Dust Settles, The Next Big Catalyst Waits In The Wings
data.description:
Alphabet Inc. aka Google (NASDAQ:GOOG, NASDAQ:GOOGL) stock has been on a decline since early February, giving up nearly a quarter of its market capitalization as I write this. Never mind that the company blew the top off all earnings estimates in Q1 2025. The rally was short-lived, which points to the possibility that investors are more concerned about the regulatory hurdle to be overcome in September than the current performance of the company. Whether it can do that and come out in one piece is debatable, with emphasis on "one piece."

As it stands this week, Alphabet is pushing back against a recent filing by the DOJ that proposes a sell-off of two critical adtech assets - DoubleClick for Publishers or DFP, and the AdX ad exchange platform. The gist of the DOJ's argument is that the bundling of these two major adtech components of its business is anti-competitive.

Alphabet's response, published on the company's blog here, is decidedly cautious. On the one side, it has proposed a list of remedial measures it is willing to take, but it has also announced its intent to appeal. The trial date is set for September, so if we go by how investors reacted to the earnings super-beat and the ensuing news flows, I'd say the stock is set to keep moving sideways until then.

My initiating thesis is that the lawsuit, while not at all immaterial to Alphabet's ad business, doesn't represent enough of a threat to current shareholders to warrant a sell-off. The recent three-month decline was partially the result of the DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK) announcement, at which time GOOG took a 4.2% haircut to its market capitalization. At a then-current valuation of $2.5 trillion, that's a hundred billion dollars. Vanished. Once again, unwarranted.

With all these haircuts, today's valuation is much more attractive, but one thing I think investors aren't really looking at is that the value of the company's various parts aren't really going to change dramatically even if this ends up in a forced divestiture. Sure, you may be issued new shares of a couple of spincos, but your investment value isn't being impacted by the divestiture. I think such a simplistic view helps bring a little clarity to the situation.

The Business Model is Being Threatened, Not the Business Itself

The market is pricing GOOG today at rock bottom levels that have never been seen.

Forget far below average. The stock recently hit all-time valuation lows, and it lingers there, tantalizing value investors who have never had a chance to jump on the GOOG bandwagon because it's been expensive for so long.

The reality of it is that the market is unable to price the stock in line with its still-strong earnings growth. That's why you'll see the stock struggling to grow while its earnings multiple keeps dropping at a much faster rate.

That divergence has been going on for the last one year or so, and it's much clearer if you zoom into that time period.

Meanwhile, revenues and earnings have kept up their decade-long upward march, going from $75 billion and $1.48 in FY15 to and $350 billion and $8.04 in FY24 for impressive CAGRs of 16.7% and 18.4%, respectively. In Q1-25, revenue grew 12% YoY and EPS grew nearly 50%, but even that positive shocker failed to move the stock back up to where it was at the start of February. That also caused the valuation to dip down to its current level, which is almost 34% lower than what it was trading for the same time last year.

Honestly speaking, I don't understand what the market is thinking right now. We seem to be exhibiting very clear signs of mass psychogenic illness, commonly known as mass hysteria. Investors have become overly news-sensitive, in my opinion. Even a cursory look at recent segment metrics should tell you that this is a solid business.

The disaggregated revenue and profitability metrics show almost flawless execution, with the only decline being a 2% dip in network earnings. This is quite significant because it's got nothing to do with Google Search, and that's what has caused the ongoing controversy between Apple's (AAPL) Eddy Cue and Alphabet.

In a nutshell, Cue claimed that Google Search volumes on the Safari browser had dipped for the first time in 22 years this April: "That has never happened in 22 years" was his claim. That led to the inevitable flurry of media reports, which in turn prompted Alphabet to respond with this.

Here's our statement on this morning’s press reports about Search traffic.

We continue to see overall query growth in Search. That includes an increase in total queries coming from Apple’s devices and platforms. More generally, as we enhance Search with new features, people are seeing that Google Search is more useful for more of their queries — and they’re accessing it for new things and in new ways, whether from browsers or the Google app, using their voice or Google Lens. We’re excited to continue this innovation and look forward to sharing more at Google I/O.

To me, what was more surprising than Cue's unexpected public claim at the antitrust trial just this past Wednesday is the fact that Alphabet felt the need to respond publicly. Apple sends a lot of traffic their way - enough to justify the $20 billion in payments to Apple every year for keeping Google has the default Safari search engine and adding a share of profit generated by associated ad campaigns. So why would Cue admit that at Alphabet's trial? I don't know the answer to that, but it's no wonder that it caused a rush of news reports. It's the classic kind of controversy that the media loves to jump on to.

For its part, Alphabet was categorically clear about search volumes, specifically from Apple's install base and platform assets. But that was already validated by Alphabet's Q1 results, so why defend a position that has been well-established? My gut instinct tells me that Alphabet is concerned that negative news will further impact its equity value.

That ties in with my hypothesis that investors are indeed more sensitive to news releases, especially negative ones.

Validation Through Valuation

So, what exactly are we getting at the current price of $155 or thereabouts? Well, the 17x P/E multiple already tells us that we're getting it at a +40% discount to the long-term average near 30x. Since Apple only breaks down the income statement down to the EBIT or operating income level, we can use that for our SOTP exercise.

First, for the whole entity.

At a 20x multiple, or the long-term average, and based on FY-24 EBIT of $112.4 billion, GOOG should carry an enterprise value of about $2.25 trillion. Its current market cap is $1.88 trillion, and with cash of $95.3 billion and total debt of $28.5 billion, that gives it an EV of $1.81 trillion for an almost 20% discount.

An SOTP valuation yields even more dismal results, if that's possible.

Google Services alone accounted for a notional EV of $2.425 trillion based on the 20x EBIT multiple. Cloud is worth another $122 billion, bringing the total to $2.55 trillion. Of course, the $15 billion in losses before interest and taxes should account for some drag on EV, about $300 billion worth, but it's clear you're getting a bargain deal for the kind of profits the company is able to generate from its two core segments - AND for the fact that it shows no sign of slowing down.

I could dig deeper and break down these segments further to assess the individual valuations of various Services units, but my take is that the more granular you get, the greater your margin of error. Besides, my simpler back-of-the-envelope calculations already proves my point very loudly.

What Should Investors Do?

Q1 earnings gave us a really good snapshot of the fundamental strength of the company, but despite the overwhelmingly bullish articles that try to validate this viewpoint, the market is very clear about its position - it is not yet ready to go back to rewarding Alphabet for growth.

Rather than asking ourselves what we can do at this point, we should be asking what we shouldn't do - and that's to sell your GOOG/GOOGL holding, or even a part of it. What you have in your portfolio is a stock injured by the media, the government, and at least one of its largest ecosystem partners, Apple.

I don't know if the right move is to Buy Alphabet now, when the market has spoken very unambiguously, but I do know that the wrong move would be to exit or cut your position. The reason I can't call Alphabet Inc. a Buy is that the possible sideways movement until the September court date brings with it an opportunity cost. Whatever money you planned to use to buy Alphabet stock at these rock-bottom valuations would do a lot better if you just stick to money markets, short-term income instruments, and other near-liquid investments. Don't Buy GOOG now, but don't Sell, either.

data.title: Sweetgreen: A Diamond In The Rough At 52-Week Lows (Upgrade)
data.description:
Sweetgreen, Inc. (NYSE:SG) can't seem to catch a break. The once highflying salad chain has been hit with a bevy of negative headlines over the past few months, from Wall Street downgrades to the departure of its COO. Now there is a lackluster Q1 earnings print that showcased very tepid sales growth impacted by a darkening macroeconomy.

Shares of Sweetgreen tumbled more than 15% to fresh 52-week lows after the Q1 print, bringing YTD losses to over 50%. While it's true that we're beginning to see signs of consumers pushing back against high prices and conserving their budgets by not eating out, I continue to believe that this company enjoys tremendous secular tailwinds. In a volatile stock market, sometimes the best investments aren't obvious: and require a keen eye that is willing to look out through years, and not just near term results.

I last wrote a neutral opinion on Sweetgreen in February, when the stock was still trading in the low $20s. I'll acknowledge that I've been frustrated by Sweetgreen's performance, and it is one of the worst performers that I've been holding in my portfolio. And yet, I believe the stock now to be dramatically oversold, as it is inching its way toward becoming a value stock once it can unlock higher margins via a growing share of Infinite Kitchens in its store portfolio. I'm upgrading my viewpoint on Sweetgreen to a buy.

Near-term results are disappointing, but we have to note that Sweetgreen isn't alone in the restaurant sector in reporting a sharp weakening of same-store sales growth as well as minor upcoming tariff impacts. Amid the carnage, here's a reminder of the longer-term bull case for Sweetgreen:

  • Secular tailwinds toward healthy eating. My thesis is that the fast food and fast casual landscape will experience a sea change over the next decade. As we continue to embrace more healthy living across all aspects of life (including and especially diet), we'll see unhealthy fast food chains shrink their footprints while healthier options thrive.
  • Infinite Kitchen overhaul. Over half of the company's new restaurant builds are planned as Infinite Kitchens, where the salad-making process is fully automated and where existing restaurants have been proven to have higher operating margins (via labor savings as well as higher throughput).
  • Menu innovation is increasing average checks and addressable market. Sweetgreen continues to tinker with its menu and release new offerings. The company notes that its avocado oil-based, air-fried Ripple Fries have become a common add-on that is increasing average check sizes, while rolling out steak has increased traffic at dinnertime.

There's also a strong value argument to be made for Sweetgreen. At current share prices near $15, Sweetgreen trades at a market cap of $1.75 billion. After we net off the $183.9 million of cash off Sweetgreen's latest balance sheet (against zero debt), the company's resulting enterprise value is $1.57 billion.

Meanwhile, for FY 2025, Sweetgreen has cut down its guidance to $740-$760 million in revenue (9-12% y/y growth), versus a 12-15% y/y growth range previously. It's expecting $30 million of adjusted EBITDA against this revenue, or a 4% margin at the midpoint.

We note that Sweetgreen's margin profile has a long ways to catch up to peers: restaurant-level profit margins, expected at 19.5% this year, lag behind peers like CAVA Group (CAVA) and Chipotle Mexican Grill (CMG) in the mid-20s. As such, it's best to look at Sweetgreen's valuation from a revenue perspective: with the implicit assumption that Infinite Kitchen overhauls will eventually help push Sweetgreen's margin profile at par or better than peers, and scale its profitability meaningfully above current levels.

Sweetgreen trades at a 2.1x EV/FY 2025 revenue multiple. As shown in the chart below, that's less than half of Chipotle's valuation, and well below Cava as well as McDonald's (MCD), which like Sweetgreen is struggling to keep same-store sales growth positive.

I also like to view Sweetgreen's valuation from a per-location perspective. The company's guidance of adding 40 stores this year implies that it will end FY 2025 with 286 locations, putting Sweetgreen's valuation at $5.5 million EV/store. Chipotle, meanwhile, has a ~$65.6 billion current enterprise value against 4,056 year-end stores (midpoint of guidance implies 330 store builds this year), or $16.2 million EV/store.

The key message here: yes, Sweetgreen stores are underperforming. But the top line pressure is due to macro conditions that are broadly impacting the whole industry (and should be temporary), while margin headwinds can hopefully be right-sized with a greater mix of automated stores in the portfolio. For investors who are willing to bank on the long term, Sweetgreen trades quite cheaply on a revenue and per-store basis.

Q1 Recap

We won't mince words: Sweetgreen's Q1 earnings print was concerning across many fronts and put the company's near-term headwinds front and center. Take a look at the Q1 earnings results below:

Sweetgreen's revenue grew just 5% y/y to $166.3 million, though this slightly beat Wall Street's expectations of $164.9 million (+4% y/y) by a one-point margin. Growth also kept pace with the 5% y/y growth rate that Sweetgreen showcased in Q4.

However, we note that the only reason Sweetgreen was able to grow at all was via the 5 net-new store openings that the company executed within the quarter. Underlying same-store sales growth (which measures the revenue growth from existing locations and excludes the contribution from newly opened restaurants), however, dipped -3.1% y/y, which was a marked 7-point deceleration from 4% growth in Q4.

The weakness in same store sales effectively confirms what Sweetgreen investors had been fearing: that inflation-strapped consumers are finally pushing back against price increases and balking at paying for $15+ salads. We do note that there were some unnatural headwinds within the quarter, including the impact of the LA wildfires in January (Sweetgreen is headquartered in Los Angeles and LA is its most concentrated market, with 24 current stores in the area plus another five in nearby Orange County). The company also called out very cold winter conditions in New York and other East Coast markets, a sentiment that many companies in the restaurant sector have echoed as impacting same-store sales growth results in Q1.

There's more going on than just weather, however. The company notes that traffic began to dip approximately concurrent with tariff announcements, defying typical spring seasonality. Per CEO Mitch Reback's remarks on the Q1 earnings call:

Now turning to fiscal year 2025. As we look out on the next three quarters, we are highly cognizant that this is a dynamic environment. As you have heard from others across our industry and seen in recent data consumer sentiment has fallen sharply. After returning to positive comp sales performance in March, we saw a mid-single digit decline in the same-store sales during April, coinciding with the tariff announcements.

Historically, April has always shown a ramp in performance as seasonal patterns kick in. This is the first time we haven’t seen that lift. We believe this is noteworthy and reflective of the volatile external environment and the softening consumer sentiment marked by more deliberate purchasing behavior and lower frequency across discretionary categories.

Our outlook assumes that the current trends continue and layers in the positive impact of upcoming menu initiatives as well as continued ramp in our new loyalty program. As we considered our fiscal year guidance, we assume no further deterioration in macroeconomic conditions. As always, our forecast is based on the information available to us today and we recognize that uncertainty in the macro environment remains elevated.

Adding insult to injury is the fact that restaurant level margins are also declining. Restaurant-level margins (which measures the profitability of stores without including corporate overhead) fell 20bps y/y to 17.9%:

The company is expecting further impact from tariffs. While food is mostly sourced domestically, the company imports paper and packaging products from China, which is expected to have a 75bps adverse impact on margins in Q2 (fading to 40bps by the end of the year, as the company has initiatives in place to source from lower-cost areas).

We still note with hope, however, that the company is still guiding to a 19.5% restaurant-level margin for the full FY 2025, implying that automation and supply chain benefits should help to lift Sweetgreen's profitability from its current trough.

Key Takeaways

Needless to say, I had hoped for much better in Sweetgreen's first quarter, and was counting on a near-term positive catalyst to reverse recent pessimism in Sweetgreen. That being said, especially with the stock having dropped ~50% since the start of the year, I'm comfortable holding Sweetgreen (and adding more on this sharp dip). Yes, we'll need to wait until macro conditions improve to see evidence of traffic growth again, but in the long run I see plenty of white space for Sweetgreen's expansion plans and its eventual improvement in margins from its investments in Infinite Kitchens as well as drive-through lanes. Stay long here.

data.title: Ford: Now A High-Yield Capital Return Play
data.description:
Despite considerable concerns about tariffs, chip export restrictions and fears about a recession, the stock market has bounced back nicely in April which also helped automaker Ford (NYSE:F). The auto maker just reported better-than-expected earnings for its first fiscal quarter, but withdrew its adjusted EBIT and free cash flow guidance for FY 2025 over the negative impact of recently announced tariffs. Shares of Ford remain interesting for investors from a valuation and a yield point of view, however. Ford's dividend yield has also risen to 7% which is significantly above the longer term average, indicating undervaluation. Ford under-performed shares of General Motors (GM) by a significant margin which I believe is not justified.

Previous rating

I had a strong buy rating on Ford’s shares ahead of the Q1’25 earnings scorecard -- Why I Am Buying The New 1-Year Lows -- largely because the company benefited from solid growth in its electric vehicle business and guided for a massive amount of free cash flow and adjusted EBIT in FY 2025. The company’s EV deliveries surged in the first-quarter, as expected, although the electric vehicle segment remained unprofitable. Because new U.S. tariffs on auto parts are increasing costs, Ford has withdrawn its guidance for FY 2025. In my opinion, the current tariff drama is only a short term distraction and I believe tariffs are not going to dramatically alter the value proposition for Ford.

Ford beats earnings for Q1’25

Ford beat estimates for both its earnings and revenues in the first-quarter: the U.S. auto giant reported adjusted earnings of $0.14 per-share which beat the average prediction by a solid margin of $0.12 per-share. The auto maker also reported $37.4B in revenue, beating the consensus estimate by $1.6B.

EV deliveries keep ramping up, but segment may be drag on Ford's earnings in FY 2025

Ford's electric vehicle business did well in the first fiscal quarter, growing deliveries and narrowing its losses on an EBIT level. Model E, which consolidates all of Ford’s electric vehicle activities in the U.S. and abroad, posted an adjusted EBIT loss of $0.8B, showing a decline rate of 38%. At the same time, the company delivered 31k electric vehicles, 213% more than in the comparable period last year. Model E will continue to be a loss-maker for Ford, however, and therefore weigh on the automaker's full-year results.

Before the earnings report was published, Ford submitted its monthly sales for April which continued to showed EV delivery momentum, driven by growing product up-take of hybrid vehicles. Ford's total electrified vehicle sales increased 8.4% year-over-year to 28,190 units. At the current run-rate, Ford should be able to least deliver 350,000 EV units (both electric and hybrid vehicles). This attractive delivery picture should also be seen in the context of a growing total addressable market for EVs which creates a long term growth opportunity for Ford.

While the market has been concerned with slowing EV growth in FY 2024, which is when big automakers have started to pull back on electric vehicle production, the outlook for growth is actually still quite positive. According to Bloomberg, the EV market is set for growth and should be able to reach, according to the International Energy Agency, to reach a total delivery volume of 2.5M electric vehicles. Ford is well positioned to capture this growth through its broad EV portfolio ranging from SUVs to commercial vans and pick-up trucks.

Tariff impact, Ford suspends FY 2025 guidance

The biggest take-away from Ford's earnings release was the auto company had to withdraw its guidance for FY 2025 which previously included the assumption that the auto maker could generate $7.0-8.5B in adjusted EBIT and $3.5-4.5B in adjusted free cash flow. The auto maker pulled its guidance due to increasing uncertainty about the final tariff bill this year. The company expects tariffs to have a negative cost impact of ~$2.5B which is projected to affect Ford negatively in the amount of $1.5B at the EBIT level.

With tariffs continuing to be renegotiated, the ultimate EBIT impact could either be higher or lower for Ford. General Motors made similar comments and revised its guidance for FY 2025 lower. Like Ford, General Motors has seen a significant increase in Q1'25 delivery volumes for its new electric vehicles which were led by the Chevrolet brand.

While the margin picture can be quite volatile for either Ford or General Motors on a quarter-over-quarter basis, I expect big U.S. auto companies to remain solidly profitable on a full-year basis in 2025. Ford's EBIT margin was 2.3% in the last quarter which compares against a margin of 8.5% for General Motors. In my opinion, Ford continues to have massive growth potential in terms of enhancing its margin profile, especially with high margin products such as the F-150 Lightning pick-up truck.

Ford's shares may now chiefly be a yield play...

Ford's full-year dividend yield has surged to more than 7% amid the recent tariff panic, creating a buying opportunity. Ford pays shareholders a regular $0.15 per-share dividend, and also paid a supplemental dividend of $0.15 per-share in Q1'25 as well. Ford has paid a special dividend for 3 years consecutively, however, which makes the 'special dividend' also recurring in nature in my opinion. The 7% yield below includes the payment of a special dividend. On a purely quarterly dividend payout, Ford's shares yield ~6%. The current yield is about 29% above the 5-year average, indicating that Ford's shares are undervalued in a historical context.

Ford could, and maybe should, be seen chiefly as a capital return play that pays investors a high recurring dividend (and likely another 'special' dividend in Q1 next year). Despite the uncertainty about Ford's EBIT potential in FY 2025, the automaker is widely free cash flow profitable and should be able to generate positive FCF on a full-year basis. In the most recent quarter, Ford's free cash flow totaled $1.86B which was 26% above the 3-year average FCF level.

Ford’s valuation

Ford is valued at a very low price-to-earnings ratio of 7.4X which is due to the fact that U.S. automakers have sold off over concerns about trade and the impact of tariffs on the earnings of U.S. auto companies. Ford's price-to-earnings ratio implies an earnings yield of 13.5% as well as a discount of 12% to the longer term valuation average. For comparison, General Motors is trading at 4.7X forward (FY 2026) earnings, implying an even higher earnings yield of 21.2%.

Shares of Ford, in my opinion, could revalue to an ~8X forward (FY 2026) price-to-earnings ratio if the auto maker manages to grow its adjusted EBIT, sees continual growth in electric vehicle deliveries and remains free cash flow-profitable. Ideally, Ford also lowers its losses in the electric vehicle segment which are also narrowing. If Ford continues to grow its EV deliveries in FY 2025 -- especially in terms of F-150 Lightning pick-up truck, Mach-E SUV and e-Transit deliveries -- I believe the auto company has revaluation potential, despite short term earnings uncertainty.

In this case, an ~8X P/E ratio implies a fair value range for Ford’s shares of at least $11.20 per-share. It should be noted, however, that earnings estimates for Ford have recently adjusted to the downside amid growing concerns about the impact of tariffs on Ford's bottom line. In the longer term, I don't believe tariffs will affect Ford's earnings potential at all and the auto company should be able to grow both its adjusted FCF and FCF in both its EV and non-EV divisions.

Risks with Ford

The biggest risk for the automaker, as I see it, relates to a slowdown in electric vehicle deliveries. While Ford’s EV segment is still losing a ton of money, the company needs this business to turn around in order to be able to have upside in consolidated EBIT and free cash flow. If the turnaround in this division falters, shares of Ford are going to have a hard time revaluing higher in 2025 and beyond. On the other hand, tariffs on U.S. vehicle imports may actually make Ford more attractive for consumers and, as a result, more profitable for investors.

Final thoughts

In my opinion, Ford is a solid deal for investors as the automaker is clearly developing in the right direction: the auto company’s electric vehicle deliveries are surging, wholesale volumes are growing and Ford should be on a good path to lower EV division losses with a higher delivery volume. The withdrawal of Ford's guidance for FY 2025 is not a game-changer and I believe tariffs are not going to fundamentally change Ford's value proposition for long term investors. With a low P/E ratio and a dividend yield crossing above 7%, Ford remains a solid bet for investors that look for both income and growth.

data.title: Stryker: Mako Spine And Shoulder Launching Is On Track (Downgrade To Hold)
data.description:
I presented my Buy thesis on Stryker Corporation (NYSE:SYK) in December 2023, pointing out the potential launch of Mako shoulder and spine applications. Since then, the stock price has increased by more than 28%, outperforming the S&P 500 index (SP500). Stryker delivered another strong quarter in Q1 FY 2025 and I trust its Mako business is well positioned for future growth. However, considering the valuation, I am downgrading to a Hold rating with a fair value of $380 per share.

Stryker posted its Q1 FY 2025 result on May 1, posting 10.1% organic revenue growth and 13.6% adj. EPS growth. As detailed in the table below, Stryker has been delivered double-digit EPS growth over the past few quarters, driven by its strong topline growth as well as margin improvement.

As shown in the table below, Stryker’s growth was across the board including instruments, endoscopy, medical, vascular, hips, knees and trauma/extremities. During the call, the management highlighted that the capital equipment market experienced strong growth with a robust order book, which indicates a strong growth ahead, in my view.

My key takeaway from the quarter was Stryker’s Mako spine and shoulder launch schedule. The management noted that Mako spine will be launched in the U.S. market in the second half of this year, and Mako shoulder will be launched in Q1 FY 2026. The company has been making solid progress on the productions and marketing preparations. I believe the new application launches will fuel Stryker’s orthopedics and spine businesses in the near future. During the call, the management expressed strong confidence in its growth in the robotics and orthopedics market.

For FY 2025, Stryker raised organic revenue growth guidance to 8.5%-9.5% organic revenue growth and reiterated its EPS guidance. The management anticipated the tariff impact will be approximately $200 million in FY 2025 based on the current tariff policies. Overall, I favor the company’s capabilities to fully offset the tariff impact and reiterate its bottom line growth guidance.

For the near-term, I forecast Stryker will deliver 9% organic revenue growth, driven by 8% growth in MedSurg and Neurotechnology and 10% growth in Orthopaedics and Spine. My growth assumption is aligned with Stryker’s long-term growth average. In addition, I anticipate Mako shoulder and spine could further enhance the company’s competitive advantages in the surgical robotics market. As discussed in my previous article, Zimmer Biomet Holdings (ZBH) has been losing market share in the hips and knees market to Stryker for many years, due to a lack of innovation and investment in R&D.

In addition, I forecast Stryker will spend 2% of revenue towards M&A, contributing an additional 110bps to the topline growth. I model 60bps annual margin expansion assuming 20bps from gross profits, 20bps from reduction in SG&A expenses and 20bps from its past reorganizations. I calculate the total operating expenses will grow by 9.1% annually, leading to a 60bps annual margin improvement.

With these assumptions, the discounted cash flow (DCF) summary:

I calculate the free cash flow from firm (FCFF) as follows:

The WACC is calculated to be 9.9% assuming: risk-free 4.2%; beta 1.03; equity risk premium 6%; cost of debt 5%; equity $146 billion; debt $13 billion; tax rate 14%. I set the terminal growth to be 4%, aligned with the long-term GDP growth rate. Discounting all the FCFF and adjusting net cash, the fair value is calculated to be $380 per share, as per my estimate.

Risks

Over the past few quarters, Stryker’s strong revenue growth has been partially driven by a strong recovery in the hospital equipment purchases. The company delivered double-digit in its capital equipment business during the quarter. However, investors should be aware that hospital equipment purchases are tied with the overall CAPEX spendings, which could be quite volatile on a quarterly basis. If hospitals have tight budgets, they might cut their annual CAPEX spendings, which could pose growth challenges to Stryker’s capital equipment business.

Conclusion

The launch of Mako spine and shoulder could potentially strengthen Stryker Corporation’s leadership in the surgical robotics market, in my view. However, considering the valuation, I am downgrading to a Hold rating with a fair value of $380 per share.

data.title: Cohort: European Security Autonomy Effort Makes This Stock A Buy
data.description:
Aerospace and defense stocks did experience some pressure this year. However, if you know where to look, there still have been plenty of opportunities with significant returns. As I focus on the aerospace and defense industries and I am Europe-based, my interest has also been to cover European aerospace and defense stocks for my readers. One of the names that I added to my coverage this year is Cohort plc (OTCPK:COHTF) (LON: CHRT) and the stock price return has been phenomenal with a 41% increase since my last report.

Important to note is that in order to assess stock price performance, I am using the listing at the London Stock Exchange, which shows a 17% increase in the stock price. In U.S. dollars, the stock price is now $19.32 exceeding my target price of $18.98.

So, this provides a good moment to discuss whether we see any upside for the stock or whether it is time to sell.

Before, I dive into the valuation to determine a stock price target, I believe it is useful to provide a description of the company’s activities sourced from my initial report. If you have already read this description or are familiar with the company, you may skip the section below and read the assessment on risks and opportunities.

Cohort Serves The U.K. And International Markets With Sensor, Communications & Intelligence Solutions

Cohort was established in 2006 to combine several smaller companies into a single group, which now employs more than 1,000 people with more than £200 million in revenues reported in FY 2024 and adjusted operating margins of 10.4%.

The group consists of seven subsidiaries since the acquisition of EM Solutions, which is a company that provides satellite communication equipment for the naval market. EM Solutions was acquired from Electro Optic Systems (OTCPK:EOPSF), which I also cover.

Its other subsidiaries are the following:

  • Chess Dynamics offers surveillance and fire control solutions.
  • EID offers advanced communications systems.
  • ELAC SONAR offers sonar systems and underwater communications.
  • MASS offers electronic warfare support, training, and data solutions.
  • MCL offers electronic communications, information systems, and signals intelligence technology.
  • SEA offers training, support, and research services for the defense and transport markets.

While the companies do not necessarily perform the same activities in each domain, I observed that the group consists of seven subsidiaries that do have some overlap. The group operates in multiple countries with EM Solutions based in Australia, EID based in Portugal, ELAC SONAR in Germany, and the other subsidiaries in the UK. This could make a potential business rationalization more complex, but I do believe that rather than solely unlocking markets as a group to boost the top line Cohort plc could also have organized into fewer subsidiaries with deeper integration. This may be challenging given that each subsidiary serves different markets, and that could provide some complexities when attempting to split operations by segment at company locations. The group is organized into two divisions, namely Communications & Intelligence and Sensors & Effectors, but it's not fully integrated in my view to unlock cost synergies.

Through its subsidiaries, Cohort plc serves the following markets:

  • Combat Systems.
  • C4ISTAR (Command, Control, Communications, Computers, Intelligence, Surveillance, Target Acquisition and Reconnaissance).
  • Data services.
  • Training and Simulation.
  • Research, advice, and support.
  • Intelligent Transport Systems.

The company’s stock trades on the London Stock Exchange under the ticker CHRT, which I believe provides the best instrument to invest in if one would like to invest in Cohort plc. Over a one-year period, the stock price has more than doubled. Cohort plc is also available on the OTC market under the COHTF ticker, but there's barely any volume, making it difficult to sell and buy at desired prices and quantities and on a desired timeframe.

What Are The Risks And Opportunities For Cohort?

While Cohort plc stock has surged since my last coverage, the reality is that the stock sold off twice in the past five months in line with the broader markets. From its lows in mid-February, the stock is now up 45%. I believe it underscores that the selloff was not necessarily driven by deep fundamental issues for Cohort but more driven by a turn in sentiment.

However, if anything, I do believe that the trade war between the U.S. and other countries has made European countries more determined to source as much defense equipment from European contractors. Also, in the context of the conflict in Ukraine, there is a major push from European countries to source from European partners. So, I would say that the opportunities are growing, and exposure to the U.S. market is not huge at just 3%.

The UK accounts for over 50% of the sales with Europe accounting for over 80% and Asia Pacific and Africa accounting for 15% of the sales. So, the business is diversified rather well. I do not see major impact from the trade war given that the U.S. only represents a small portion of sales. Apart from that, defense budgets are expanding globally, providing a positive backdrop.

With trade policy evolving almost every week, there is a degree of uncertainty and while the impact is not direct, we should keep in mind there may be currency fluctuations involved.

A risk to the stock price, not necessarily the fundamentals, would be a ceasefire in Ukraine that could somewhat subdue investor sentiment and lead to pressure on the stock price. However, realistically I do not believe that this would be justified as a ceasefire in Ukraine does not remove an adversary on the borders of Europe.

Cohort plc Stock Upgraded To Buy On European Security Autonomy Effort

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and update the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

Note: The price targets are based on the ticker at the London Stock Exchange converted to USD.

Initially, I had a hold rating on Cohort plc, but I do believe that with a push in Europe for security autonomy the market is looking increasingly more positive for defense contractors such as Cohort.

EBITDA is set to grow more than 20% annually. We did not see any major changes to EBITDA estimates other than a tailwind when converting the estimates from GBP to USD. Free cash flow will increase roughly 17% annually, but the overall increase in FCF generation has increased by around 6% reflecting the change in the GBP/USD exchange rate. This actually means that there is a small downward revision in 2025 and 2026 driven by capital expenditures.

In my prior report, I did mark an increase in the stock price to trade in line with peers would provide significant upside, and that is also what happened. For 2025, there is not much upside, but that also should not come as a surprise. Cohort’s financial year ends in April, so we have to look at the target for 2026 and that provides a stock price target of $24.15 or 26% upside. With the hold rating, we might have missed the run-up towards a peer group valuation, but I am upgrading to buy as forward earnings provide substantial upside.

Conclusion: Cohort plc Has Upside On A Peer Group Valuation

I believe that with the increase in the stock prices, we have seen that the market is valuing European defense stocks such as Cohort in line with the peer group, and that definitely has not always been the case. However, now that a peer group valuation seems to have become the standard for many European names, I do believe that the stock is a buy with a $24.15 price target. I do not believe there are major adverse impacts for Cohort from the trade turmoil, and globally we are seeing escalations in conflict zones while peace in Ukraine is still not happening, and I would be negatively surprised if a ceasefire would also reduce Europe’s push towards security autonomy. So, trying to juggle all moving elements, I do believe that the stock is a buy.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
